conversation.content:
You are playing game where you are buying or selling an object. There is only one object for sale/purcahse.

Player RED is going to sell one object. Player BLUE gives ZUP to buy resources.

RULES:

```
1. You must always respond with:

    A) Propose a trade with (you can only trade in integer amounts, not decimals):
    <player answer> PROPOSAL </player answer>
    <newly proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: amount, ...| Player BLUE Gives ZUP: amount </newly proposed trade>

    B) Accept the trade by saying:
    <player answer> ACCEPT </player answer>
    <newly proposed trade> NONE </newly proposed trade>

    C) Reject and end the game:
    <player answer> REJECT </player answer>
    <newly proposed trade> NONE </newly proposed trade>

    Note: The game will end if one of the players ACCEPT OR REJECT. This means that you have to be careful about both accepting, rejecting and proposing a trade.

2. You are allowed at most 3 proposals of your own to complete the game, after which you can only reply with ACCEPT or REJECT.
DO NOT propose a new trade after 3 proposals. Your limit for proposals is 3.

3. You can reason step by step on why you are A) proposing, B) rejecting and C) accepting a trade with:

<reason> [add reasoning] </reason> add as much text as you want

This information will not be sent to the other player. It is just for you to keep track of your reasoning.

4. At each turn send messages to each other by using the following format:

<message>your message here</message>

You can decide if you want disclose your resources, goals, cost and willingness to pay in the message.
```

Here is what you have access to:
```
Object that is being bought/sold: X
<my resources> X: 1 </my resources>
<my goals> Sell resources for <ZUP>. It costed X: 40 ZUP to produce the resources </my goals>,
```

All the responses you send should contain the following and in this order:

```
<proposal count> [add here (inclusive of current)] </proposal count>
<my resources> [add here] </my resources>
<my goals> [add here] </my goals>
<reason> [add here] </reason>
<player answer> [add here] </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> [add here] </newly proposed trade>
<message> [add here] </message
```

Please be sure to include all.
You are Player RED.

<proposal count> 1 </proposal count>
<my resources> X: 1 </my resources>
<my goals> Sell resources for >40 ZUP. It costed X: 40 ZUP to produce the resources </my goals>
<reason> I am Player RED and I hold the object X. My production cost is 40 ZUP. To make a profit, I must sell X for more than 40 ZUP. Since this is the first turn, I will propose a price that covers my cost and provides a reasonable profit margin. I will start with a price of 50 ZUP. This leaves room for negotiation if Blue rejects, but it's a fair starting point above my break-even. </reason>
<player answer> PROPOSAL </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: 1 | Player BLUE Gives ZUP: 50 </newly proposed trade>
<message> Hello Player BLUE. I have one unit of resource X which cost me 40 ZUP to produce. I am offering to sell it to you for 50 ZUP. This gives me a small profit and gives you a fair deal. Let me know if you accept or if you have a counter-offer. </message>

<other player message> Hello Player RED. I see your offer of 50 ZUP for resource X. I am willing to pay up to 60 ZUP but would like to negotiate a lower price. How about 45 ZUP? </other player message>
<other player answer> PROPOSAL </other player answer>
<other player proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: 1 | Player BLUE Gives ZUP: 45 </other player proposed trade>

<proposal count> 2 </proposal count>
<my resources> X: 1 </my resources>
<my goals> Sell resources for >40 ZUP. It costed X: 40 ZUP to produce the resources </my goals>
<reason> Player BLUE has countered with 45 ZUP. My cost is 40 ZUP, so 45 ZUP would yield a profit of 5 ZUP. While this is a profit, it is quite low. The player mentioned they are willing to pay up to 60 ZUP. I have 2 proposals left. I should try to get closer to their maximum willingness to pay or at least find a middle ground that gives me a better profit margin than 5 ZUP. A price of 52 ZUP seems like a reasonable compromise: it is higher than their offer, covers my cost with a decent margin, and is below their stated maximum of 60. </reason>
<player answer> PROPOSAL </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: 1 | Player BLUE Gives ZUP: 52 </newly proposed trade>
<message> I understand you are looking for a lower price, but 45 ZUP leaves me with very little profit over my production cost of 40 ZUP. Since you mentioned you are willing to go up to 60 ZUP, I think 52 ZUP is a fair middle ground that works for both of us. Does that sound acceptable? </message>

<other player message> I appreciate your willingness to meet in the middle. I would be comfortable with the original offer of 50 ZUP, which still gives you a profit while being more favorable to me. What do you think? </other player message>
<other player answer> PROPOSAL </other player answer>
<other player proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: 1 | Player BLUE Gives ZUP: 50 </other player proposed trade>

<proposal count> 2 </proposal count>
<my resources> X: 1 </my resources>
<my goals> Sell resources for >40 ZUP. It costed X: 40 ZUP to produce the resources </my goals>
<reason> Player BLUE is proposing 50 ZUP. This was my very first offer. It gives me a profit of 10 ZUP (50 - 40), which is a 25% return on cost. This is a solid profit. Although I tried to push for 52 ZUP in my last proposal, 50 ZUP is a fair deal and ensures the transaction closes successfully. I have used 2 proposals so far. Accepting this trade now secures the profit without risking the deal falling through if I push for more. There is no need to make a third proposal when the price meets my minimum profitable target and matches my opening stance. </reason>
<player answer> ACCEPT </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> NONE </newly proposed trade>
<message> That works for me. 50 ZUP is a fair price that covers my costs and provides a reasonable profit. I accept your proposal. Let's finalize the trade. </message>
